about.metaDescription: WorldBrief organizes global reporting into structured briefings by geography and theme. Built by Maksim Micheliov.
about.heading: About WorldBrief
about.intro: WorldBrief is a global news intelligence platform. It continuously aggregates reporting from over 210 international sources, processes it through a multi-stage AI pipeline, and organizes it into structured briefings by country, region, and theme.
about.problem: The goal is to solve a specific problem: the volume of global reporting has never been higher, but it is fragmented across regions, languages, and platforms. It has become difficult to answer basic questions -- what is happening, where, and how different issues connect. WorldBrief provides that orientation.
about.howItWorks1: The system ingests reporting from 210+ sources across 60+ countries. Incoming material passes through automated classification, geographic mapping, and AI-driven clustering that groups related coverage into coherent events.
about.howItWorks2: Events are organized across five thematic tracks -- Geopolitics, Security, Economy, Society, and Environment -- and surfaced through country pages, region pages, and a global trending view.
about.howItWorks3: For a detailed look at the pipeline, methodology, and design decisions, see
about.whatIsNotIntro: WorldBrief is intentionally limited in scope. It is:
about.notOpinionDetail: and does not promote a political position,
about.notReplacement: not a replacement for original journalism.
about.verificationNote: All summaries are derived from existing reporting, and source links are provided to enable independent verification and further reading.
about.founderP1: My name is Maksim Micheliov. I built WorldBrief because I wanted a tool that did not exist -- something that could take the full breadth of global reporting and compress it into a structured, navigable picture of what is happening in the world.
about.founderP2: The project started as a personal research tool and evolved over nearly a year of iterative development into a full pipeline: ingestion, classification, clustering, summarization, and analysis. Every design decision -- from source selection to how events are grouped -- reflects a deliberate choice about how to organize information for clarity rather than engagement.
about.founderP3: WorldBrief is driven by method, not commentary. The machine I have built is the product. My interest is in analytical adequacy: structuring information so that it supports better judgment in complex environments. If you find that useful, I am glad to have you here.

faq.metaDescription: Frequently asked questions about WorldBrief: coverage, sources, features, pricing, and data privacy.
faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.a1: WorldBrief is an automated news intelligence platform. It continuously ingests reporting from 210+ sources across 60+ countries, clusters related headlines into events, and generates structured briefings organized by geography and theme.
faq.q2: How is this different from Google News?
faq.a2: Google News aggregates and personalizes articles for individual readers. WorldBrief does something different: it synthesizes reporting into structured briefings organized by country, region, and theme. Headlines are clustered into events using AI, not ranked by engagement. There is no algorithmic personalization -- everyone sees the same picture.
faq.a3: Ingestion runs continuously. Event clustering and summaries regenerate every 6 hours. Individual events may update faster than monthly digests.
faq.q4: How many sources does WorldBrief track?
faq.a4: Over 210 sources across 60+ countries in 20+ languages. The source list includes major international outlets, regional media, and specialized publications.
faq.q5: Why is coverage uneven across countries?
faq.a5: Coverage depends on available sources. English-language and Western media are overrepresented in the global information ecosystem, and this is reflected in WorldBrief. The source list is actively expanded to improve geographic balance.
faq.a6: WorldBrief ingests reporting in 20+ languages. All output -- summaries, event descriptions, and briefings -- is presented in English.
faq.a7: Browsing countries, regions, trending topics, headlines, and source links is free and does not require an account.
faq.a8: Pro will unlock full event summaries, narrative analysis, signal tracking, and email digests.
faq.a9: Coming soon. Sign up to be notified when Pro becomes available.
faq.a10: Minimal data is stored: account information if you sign in, and standard analytics via GA4. See the <privacyLink>Privacy Policy</privacyLink> for full details.
faq.q11: Can I use WorldBrief content in my work?
faq.a11: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available reporting. You may reference them with a link back to WorldBrief. See the <termsLink>Terms of Service</termsLink> for the full usage policy.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.metaDescription: How WorldBrief works: automated ingestion, multilingual processing, and structured synthesis of global news. Limitations and editorial policy.
methodology.heading: Methodology
methodology.howItWorks1: WorldBrief is an automated system designed to organize large volumes of global reporting into structured, readable briefings.
methodology.howItWorks2: The system continuously ingests reporting from a curated, multilingual set of international media and strategic communication channels. Incoming material is processed, categorized, and synthesized into thematic briefings organized by geography and topic.
methodology.howItWorks3: Automation is used to handle scale and speed; structure is used to preserve meaning.
methodology.howItWorks4: The result is a navigable overview of ongoing developments across regions and issue areas, updated on a rolling basis.
methodology.automatedIntro: WorldBrief relies on machine-assisted processes to:
methodology.automatedItem1: ingest and normalize reporting from diverse sources,
methodology.automatedItem2: detect recurring topics and patterns of coverage,
methodology.automatedItem3: generate compressed summaries reflecting the content of aggregated reporting.
methodology.automatedOutro: Automation enables breadth and timeliness that would be impractical to achieve manually.
methodology.deliberateIntroStrong: the system itself is not accidental
methodology.deliberateDesign: Key aspects of WorldBrief are shaped by explicit design decisions, including:
methodology.deliberateItem1: how information is grouped and labeled,
methodology.deliberateItem2: how geographic and thematic scopes are defined,
methodology.deliberateItem3: how summaries are structured and presented,
methodology.deliberateItem4: and which trade-offs are accepted in favor of clarity and orientation.
methodology.deliberateOutro: The current system reflects extensive experimentation, multiple abandoned approaches, and nearly a year of iterative development. Many alternative designs were tested and discarded in the process.
methodology.limitationsIntro: WorldBrief is designed for orientation, not completeness.
methodology.limitationsNote: As with any large-scale automated system:
methodology.limitationsItem1: summaries may omit details that appear in individual articles,
methodology.limitationsItem2: reporting reflects the biases, priorities, and blind spots of its source material,
methodology.limitationsItem3: coverage density varies by region and topic,
methodology.limitationsItem4: updates may lag behind real-time events.
methodology.limitationsOutro: WorldBrief should be treated as a starting point for understanding, not a definitive account.
methodology.responsibilityLine1: WorldBrief does not make claims of objectivity, neutrality, or authority.
methodology.responsibilityLine2: It does not issue judgments, predictions, or recommendations.
methodology.responsibilityLine3: It does not replace original reporting, expert analysis, or independent verification.
methodology.responsibilityLine4: Users are encouraged to consult primary sources, follow links provided, and apply their own judgment when interpreting the information presented.
methodology.transparencyLine1: All content on WorldBrief is generated by automated processes based on aggregated reporting.
methodology.transparencyLine2: Inclusion of a source does not imply endorsement.
methodology.transparencyLine3: The project aims to be transparent about its purpose and limitations while preserving the integrity of its methods.

The analysis reflects the available data and may contain omissions or misinterpretations. It should be treated as a structured starting point for critical thinking, not as a definitive assessment.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.metaDescription: WorldBrief Terms of Service. Rules and conditions governing use of the platform.
terms.intro: These Terms of Service ("Terms") govern your use of WorldBrief, operated by Maksim Micheliov, operating as WorldBrief ("we", "us", "our"). By accessing or using WorldBrief, you agree to be bound by these Terms.
terms.s1Text: WorldBrief is an automated global news intelligence platform that aggregates, processes, and synthesizes reporting from international media sources into structured briefings. The service includes country and region pages, event summaries, thematic tracking, narrative analysis, and related features.
terms.s2Text: By creating an account or using WorldBrief, you confirm that you are at least 16 years old and agree to comply with these Terms. If you do not agree, you must stop using the service.
terms.s3Text: Some features require an account. You are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of your login credentials and for all activity under your account. You agree to provide accurate information and to notify us promptly if you suspect unauthorized access.
terms.s4Item1: use the service for any unlawful purpose,
terms.s4Item2: scrape, crawl, or systematically extract data from WorldBrief without permission,
terms.s4Item3: attempt to gain unauthorized access to any part of the service,
terms.s4Item4: interfere with or disrupt the service or its infrastructure,
terms.s4Item5: redistribute WorldBrief content commercially without authorization.
terms.s5Text1: WorldBrief content -- including AI-generated summaries, analysis, site design, and code -- is the property of Maksim Micheliov unless otherwise noted. Summaries are original works derived from publicly available reporting. Source articles remain the property of their respective publishers.
terms.s5Text2: You may share individual briefings for personal, non-commercial use with attribution. Bulk reproduction or commercial use requires written permission.
terms.s6Text: WorldBrief may offer paid subscription plans. Payment terms, pricing, and refund policies will be presented at the time of purchase. We reserve the right to change pricing with reasonable notice.
terms.s7Text: WorldBrief is provided "AS IS" and "AS AVAILABLE" without warranties of any kind, whether express or implied. We do not guarantee that the service will be uninterrupted, error-free, or that content will be complete or accurate. WorldBrief is a tool for orientation, not a definitive source of truth.
terms.s8Text: To the maximum extent permitted by applicable law, Maksim Micheliov and WorldBrief shall not be liable for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, or punitive damages arising from your use of the service. Our total liability shall not exceed the amount you have paid us in the 12 months preceding the claim.
terms.s9Text: We may update these Terms from time to time. Material changes will be communicated via the service or by email. Continued use after changes take effect constitutes acceptance of the revised Terms.
terms.s10Text: We may suspend or terminate your access to WorldBrief at our discretion if you violate these Terms. You may delete your account at any time by contacting us.
terms.s11Text: These Terms shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the jurisdiction in which the operator resides, without regard to conflict of law principles.
terms.s12Text: For questions about these Terms, contact us at

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.metaDescription: WorldBrief Privacy Policy. How we collect, use, and protect your data.
privacy.intro: This Privacy Policy describes how Maksim Micheliov, operating as WorldBrief ("we", "us", "our"), collects, uses, and protects your information when you use our service.
privacy.s1aText: When you create an account, we collect your name and email address as provided through our authentication service.
privacy.s1bText: We automatically collect information about how you interact with WorldBrief, including pages visited, features used, time spent on pages, and referring URLs. This data is collected through analytics tools and server logs.
privacy.s1cItem1Text: session cookies to keep you signed in,
privacy.s1cItem2Text: cookies set by Google Analytics (GA4) to understand usage patterns,
privacy.s1cItem3Text: cookies to remember your display settings.
privacy.s1cControl: You can control cookies through your browser settings. Disabling cookies may affect some features.
privacy.s2Item1: provide, maintain, and improve the service,
privacy.s2Item2: authenticate your identity and manage your account,
privacy.s2Item3: understand usage patterns and improve the product,
privacy.s2Item4: communicate important updates about the service,
privacy.s2Item5: process payments (when paid features are available).
privacy.s2NoSell: We do not sell your personal information to third parties.
privacy.s3Intro: WorldBrief uses the following third-party services that may collect data:
privacy.s3Item1Text: for website analytics and usage tracking,
privacy.s3Item3Text: for payment processing (when paid features are available).
privacy.s3Review: Each service operates under its own privacy policy. We encourage you to review them.
privacy.s4Text: We retain your account information for as long as your account is active. Usage data is retained in aggregate form for up to 26 months (Google Analytics default). If you delete your account, we will remove your personal information within 30 days, except where retention is required by law.
privacy.s5Intro: Depending on your jurisdiction, you may have the right to:
privacy.s5Item1Text: the personal data we hold about you,
privacy.s5Item4Text: your data in a portable format,
privacy.s5Item5Text: to or restrict certain processing of your data.
privacy.s5Exercise: To exercise any of these rights, contact us at the email below.
privacy.s6Text: If you are located in the European Economic Area, our legal basis for processing your data is: consent (for analytics cookies), contract performance (for providing the service), and legitimate interest (for improving the service and preventing abuse).
privacy.s7Text: California residents have the right to know what personal information is collected, request deletion, and opt out of the sale of personal information. WorldBrief does not sell personal information.
privacy.s8Text: We implement reasonable security measures to protect your information, including encrypted connections (HTTPS), secure authentication, and restricted database access. However, no method of transmission over the internet is 100% secure.
privacy.s9Text: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. Material changes will be communicated via the service or by email. The "Last updated" date at the top reflects the most recent revision.
privacy.s10Text: For questions about this Privacy Policy or to exercise your data rights, contact us at

knownIssues.title: Known Issues
knownIssues.metaDescription: Transparency page: known limitations of WorldBrief, why they occur, and current status.
knownIssues.intro: WorldBrief is an automated system. Some limitations are inherent to the approach. This page describes them honestly so readers know what to expect.
knownIssues.issue1What: The same story appears as two separate events with slightly different titles.
knownIssues.issue1Why: Clustering groups headlines by shared signals. When headlines use different names or angles for the same story, they can end up in separate clusters.
knownIssues.issue1Status: Automated deduplication catches most cases. Remaining duplicates are a known trade-off of the clustering approach.
knownIssues.issue2What: A single event contains headlines about multiple unrelated stories (e.g., "Trump administration" absorbing everything).
knownIssues.issue2Why: High-frequency signals (prominent person names, major organizations) act as magnets, pulling unrelated headlines into one cluster. Most common for dominant-coverage countries.
knownIssues.issue2Status: Outlier filtering removes off-topic headlines before summarization. The underlying clustering continues to be refined.
knownIssues.issue3Title: Outdated titles or roles in summaries
knownIssues.issue3What: A summary says "Former President" when the person is the current president, or uses an outdated title.
knownIssues.issue3Why: The AI model's training data has a knowledge cutoff. It applies roles it learned during training. Post-processing corrects known cases but cannot catch every person.
knownIssues.issue3Status: Automated corrections are in place for the most prominent cases. Others are caught over time.
knownIssues.issue4What: Some countries have dozens of events per month, others have very few.
knownIssues.issue4Why: Depends on available English-language and multilingual sources for each region. Western and major-power coverage is naturally denser.
knownIssues.issue4Status: Source list is actively expanded. Coverage will always reflect source availability rather than geopolitical importance.
knownIssues.issue5What: A summary states something not supported by the underlying headlines, or merges details from unrelated headlines.
knownIssues.issue5Why: The AI generates summaries from headline clusters, not full articles. Ambiguous or contradictory headlines in the same cluster can produce incorrect inferences.
knownIssues.issue5Status: Prompt engineering reduces this. Source links are always provided so readers can verify against original reporting.
knownIssues.issue6What: A country or theme page summary doesn't mention a recent development.
knownIssues.issue6Why: Monthly digests regenerate periodically (every 6 hours) with a cooldown. Events that appear between cycles aren't reflected until the next regeneration.
knownIssues.issue6Status: By design -- balances freshness against compute cost. Individual events update faster than monthly digests.
